q3: The recurring issue was tonal calibration — the model kept reaching for rhetorical emphasis that the evidence didn't warrant. It wasn't wrong about the substance, but it kept editorializing in ways that required me to pull it back. The asymmetry framing eventually landed, but it took a round of explicit correction to get there.
q5: I looked at it, yes. It captured most of what I care about — the graduated recommendations criterion and the inline translation rule are both right, and the urgency-without-alarmism framing is accurate. What it didn't fully capture is how easily a draft can satisfy each criterion individually while still reading as advocacy in aggregate — the rubric checks boxes but doesn't account for how the pieces interact.
q6: The rubric made me notice that the model was consistently stronger on structure than on register — it knew where to put things but kept misjudging how much weight the language should carry. Seeing it laid out, I think the model treats 'urgency without alarmism' as a content rule when it's really a tone rule, and those aren't the same thing.

q6: The rubric made me notice that the model is genuinely good at structural scaffolding — the headers and section flow were clean throughout — but it exposed that the model sometimes prioritizes showing its work over just doing the work. q3: The middle paragraph was the persistent sticking point — the model kept building strong evidentiary groundwork but deferring the conclusion, handing off to the next section instead of landing its own directive. q5: I looked at it, and honestly it captured most of what I care about reasonably well — the graduated recommendations criterion and the principle-example-recommendation structure both reflect how I actually think about these documents. What it didn't fully capture is how the middle paragraph's problem wasn't structural in an abstract sense but specifically about a paragraph that earned its verdict and then refused to deliver it. The rubric gave the model the right framework but couldn't substitute for the specific feedback about where the logic was stalling.
q6: The rubric made me notice that the model was consistently strong on the opening and closing sections — the human impact framing and the urgency-without-alarmism tone came through early and held — while the middle argument section was where it kept losing the thread. That pattern wasn't obvious to me in the moment because I was focused on the specific sentence-level problem, but stepping back I can see the model's structural instinct was sound; it just had trouble closing individual argument units before moving on.
